---
title: "Workshop 2 - Advanced data wrangling"
author: "Tio"
format: html
editor: visual
---

## Quarto

Quarto enables you to weave together content and executable code into a finished document. To learn more about Quarto see <https://quarto.org>.

## 2.2 Tidying data

```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```

#example dataset compute rate per 10,000

```{r}
table1 %>%
  mutate(rate = cases / population * 10000) #Tible is a modern version of data frame
```

#compute cases per year

```{r}
table1 %>%
  count (year, wt = cases)
```

#visualize the change in cases over time

```{r}
ggplot(table1, aes(x = year, y = cases)) +
  geom_line( aes(group = country), color = "grey50") +
  geom_point( aes(color = country))
```

pivot the data to a tidy format using two functions: pivot_longer() and pivot_wider() pivot_longer() is used to transform data from a wide format to a long format, pivot_wider() is used to transform data from a long format to a wide format.

```{r}
tidyr:pivot_longer()
tidyr:pivot_wider()
```

```{r}
billboard |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = starts_with("wk"),
    names_to = "week",
    values_to = "rank"
  )
```

cols: specifies the columns to pivot, in this case, all columns that start with "wk". names_to: specifies the name of the new column that will contain the names of the original columns, in this case, "week". values_to: specifies the name of the new column that will contain the values from the original columns, in this case, "rank".

#Before

```{r}

df <- tribble(
  ~id,  ~bp1, ~bp2,
  "A",  100,  120,
  "B",  140,  115,
  "C",  120,  125
)

```

#After we want to tidy the data by pivoting the bp1 and bp2 columns into a longer format

```{r}
df |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols =bp1:bp2,
    names_to = "measurement",
    values_to = "value"
  )
```

We’ll use an example from R4DS to explore pivot_wider() looking at the cms_patient_experience dataset from the Centers of Medicare and Medicaid

```{r}
cms_patient_experience

cms_patient_experience |>
  distinct(measure_cd, measure_title)

#
cms_patient_experience |> 
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = measure_cd,
    values_from = prf_rate
  )

#
cms_patient_experience |> 
  pivot_wider(
    id_cols = starts_with("org"),
    names_from = measure_cd,
    values_from = prf_rate
  )
```

Pivoting wider - example

```{r}
df <- tribble(
  ~id,  ~measurement, ~value,
  "A",  "bp1", 100,
  "B",  "bp1", 140,
  "B",  "bp2", 115,
  "A",  "bp2", 120,
  "A",  "bp3", 105,
)
```

#take the names from the measurement column using the names_from() argument and the values from the value column using the values_from() argument:

```{r}
df |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = measurement,
    values_from = value
  )

df |> 
  distinct(measurement) |> 
  pull()

df |> 
  select(-measurement, -value) |> 
  distinct()

```

pivot_wider() then combines these results to generate an empty dataframe:

```{r}
df |> 
  select(-measurement, -value) |> 
  distinct() |> 
  mutate(x = NA, y = NA, z = NA)
```

#2.6 Pivoting Exercises using Palmer Penguins

```{r}
library(palmerpenguins)
```

# Create a long version of the penguins dataset

```{r}
penguins_long <- penguins |>
  pivot_longer(
    cols = c(bill_length_mm, bill_depth_mm, flipper_length_mm, body_mass_g),
    names_to = "measurement_type",
    values_to = "value"
  )
```

# View the result

```{r}
head(penguins_long)
```

#visualize the distribution of each measurement type across species using ggplot2

```{r}
penguins_long |>
  drop_na(value) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = value, fill = species)) +
  geom_histogram(bins = 30, alpha = 0.7, colour = "black") +
  facet_wrap(~ measurement_type, scales = "free_x") +
  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Morphometric distributions across penguin species",
    x = "Measurement value",
    y = "Frequency"
  )
```

#Widening data with pivot_wider() First, let's calculate the mean body mass for each species on each island using group_by() and summarise().

```{r}
mass_summary <- penguins |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species, island) |>
  summarise(mean_mass = mean(body_mass_g))

head(mass_summary)
```

#pull the island names up into columns, creating a clean matrix of species by island
```{r}
mass_matrix <- mass_summary |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = island,
    values_from = mean_mass
  )

head(mass_matrix)
```

#2.7 Separating and uniting data tables

```{r}
table3
# we want to seperate the rate column into two variabes 1) cases and 2) population

table3 %>%
  separate(rate, into = c("cases", "population"))
#or
table3 %>%
  separate(rate, into = c("cases", "population"), sep = "/")

#use separate() to convert them to better types using convert = TRUE. Now you can see they are listed as integer types(<int>)
table3 %>%
  separate(rate, into = c("cases", "population"), convert = TRUE)

#separate() can also be used to split the year column into two variables: century and year. The sep argument specifies that the split should occur after the second character.
table3 %>% 
  separate(year, into = c("century", "year"), sep = 2)

#Using unite()
table5 %>% 
  unite(new, century, year, sep = "")

```
#2.8 Wrangling strings and dates
Standardising text with stringr
string package begin with str_
```{r}
library(tidyverse)
```

# A remarkably messy data frame of field sites
```{r}
messy_sites <- tibble(
  site_id = c(" Nelly Bay", "nelly_bay", "NELLY BAY", " Geoffrey_Bay ", "geoffrey bay")
)
```

# Using stringr within mutate to standardize the text
```{r}

clean_sites <- messy_sites |>
  mutate(
    # 1. Convert everything to lowercase
    site_clean = str_to_lower(site_id),
    # 2. Replace any spaces with underscores
    site_clean = str_replace_all(site_clean, pattern = " ", replacement = "_"),
    # 3. Trim any leading or trailing whitespace (invisible spaces at the ends)
    site_clean = str_trim(site_clean)
  )
print(clean_sites)
```
For high-frequency marine data, such as acoustic telemetry or CTD profiles, 
we often need to parse exact timestamps. We do this by appending _hms (hours, minutes, seconds) to our functions.

# A tibble of raw sensor data with a messy character timestamp
```{r}
sensor_data <- tibble(
  raw_time = c("14-05-2026 08:30:00", "14-05-2026 08:45:00", "14-05-2026 09:00:00"),
  temperature = c(24.5, 24.6, 24.4)
)
```

# Converting character strings to true POSIXct datetime objects

```{r}
sensor_clean <- sensor_data |>
  mutate(
    true_time = dmy_hms(raw_time)
  )
print(sensor_clean)
```
#2.9 Relational data: Joining tables
Table 1: Biological observation data
```{r}
observations <- tibble(
  site_code = c("NB", "GB", "MI", "NB", "HB"),
  species = c("Trout", "Snapper", "Trout", "Cod", "Trout"),
  count = c(5, 2, 1, 3, 8)
)

```

Table 2: Spatial metadata
```{r}
site_metadata <- tibble(
  site_code = c("NB", "GB", "MI", "RP", "WP"),
  zone = c("Marine National Park", "Conservation Park", "Habitat Protection", "General Use", "Other Use"),
  lat = c(-19.16, -19.15, -19.14, -19.12, -19.11)
)
```

# Joining metadata to our observations

```{r}
joined_data <- observations |>
  left_join(site_metadata, by = join_by(site_code))

print(joined_data)

matched_data <- observations |>
  inner_join(site_metadata, by = join_by(site_code))
glimpse(matched_data)
print(matched_data)
```
# Which observations are missing from our metadata dictionary?
```{r}
missing_context <- observations |>
  anti_join(site_metadata, by = join_by(site_code))

print(missing_context)
```
#left_join() save all the rows from the left table (observations) and adds matching columns from the right table (site_metadata). If there is no match, it fills with NA.

#inner_join() only keeps rows where there is a match in both tables, so it will exclude any observations that don't have corresponding metadata.

#anti_join() returns rows from the left table (observations) that do not have a match in the right table (site_metadata), which helps us identify observations that are missing metadata context.

#2.10 Handling missing values
#Converting legacy errors with na_if()

# Raw data from an old temperature logger
```{r}
logger_data <- tibble(
  depth_m = c(10, 20, 30, 40),
  temp_c = c(24.5, 24.1, -999, 23.5)) # -999 is a known sensor error code
```

# Convert the -999 error codes to true NA values

```{r}
fixed_logger <- logger_data |>
  mutate(temp_c = na_if(temp_c, -999))

print(fixed_logger)
```
##Replacing missing values with coalesce()

```{r}
# Simulate some count data
shark_counts <- tibble(
  site = c("Reef_A", "Reef_B", "Reef_C"),
  shark_count = c(3, NA, 5)) # The NA here actually means 0 sharks were seen
# Replace NA with 0 
shark_fixed <- shark_counts |>
  mutate(shark_count = coalesce(shark_count, 0))
print(shark_fixed)

#The special case of NaN (Not a Number)
cpue_data <- tibble(
  site = c("bay1", "bay2"),
  catch = c(10, 0),
  effort_hours = c(2, 0)
)
# Calculate CPUE (catch per unit effort) 
cpue_calc <- cpue_data |>
  mutate(
    cpue = catch / effort_hours)
print(cpue_calc)

#Implicit missing values and the zero-data framework: complete()
raw_catch <- tibble(
  site = c("Reef_1", "Reef_1", "Reef_2"),
  species = c("Pmaculatus", "Pleopardus", "Pmaculatus"),
  count = c(5, 2, 8))

#The nuclear option: drop_na()
sensor_log <- tibble(
  day = 1:4,
  salinity = c(35.2, 35.1, NA, 35.3)
)

 clean_log <- sensor_log |>
  drop_na(salinity)
```

#2.11 Practical Exercises: Penguins, Pivots, and Relational Data
#Exercise 1: Cleaning the messy metadata

#Exercise 1: Cleaning the messy metadata

```{r}
# Make sure packages are loaded
 library(tidyverse)
 library(palmerpenguins)
 library(lubridate)
 
```

```{r}
 # The messy metadata provided by your colleague
 island_metadata <- tibble(
   island_name = c(" biscoe", "Dream ", "Torgersen"),
   station_install = c("15/01/2003", "22-03-2004", "05/11/2001"),
   latitude = c(-64.81, -64.73, -64.76)
 )
 
 print(island_metadata)
 
```


# Clean the metadata
# Make sure packages are loaded
First, we remove extra spaces from the island names and standardise the text format.
Then, we convert the station installation dates into a proper date format so they can be used reliably in later analysis.
```{r}
clean_metadata <- island_metadata |>
  mutate(
    island_name = str_trim(island_name),
    island_name = str_to_title(island_name),
    station_install = dmy(station_install)
  )

clean_metadata
```


# Exercise 2: Relational join
```{r}
penguins_spatial <- penguins |>
  left_join(
    clean_metadata,
    by = join_by(island == island_name)
  )

head(penguins_spatial)
```

#exercise 3: the wide summary matrix
table showing the maximum body mass recorded for each species, separated by island

```{r}
max_mass_matrix <- penguins_spatial |>
  drop_na(body_mass_g) |>
  group_by(species, island) |>
  summarise(
    max_body_mass = max(body_mass_g),
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  pivot_wider(
    names_from = island,
    values_from = max_body_mass
  )
max_mass_matrix
  
```
